Resolv 黑客攻击：一把被盗密钥如何印出 2300 万美元

The Resolv hack: How one compromised key printed $23M

www.chainalysis.com 作者 timbowhite 分数 85 评论 107 2026/03/24 06:01
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 2026 年 3 月 22 日，Resolv DeFi 协议成为最新的例子，说明当安全假设失效时，DeFi 中的问题会以多么快的速度崩溃。在几分钟内，攻击者就能够铸造数千万个 Resolv 的无支持稳定币 (USR)，并提取大约 2300 万美元的价值，引发急剧的脱钩并迫使协议停止运营。乍一看，这可能看起来像是另一个智能合约漏洞。但事实并非如此。该代码完全按照预期工作。相反，这是过度信任链下基础设施的情况。随着 DeFi 系统变得更加复杂并使用更多的外部服务、特权密钥和云基础设施，攻击面远远超出了区块链本身。在这篇文章中，我们将看看发生了什么以及影响是什么。我们还将探讨当链下组件受到损害时，只有实时的链上威胁检测和响应机制才能充当关键的最后一道防线，并区分受控制的事件和价值数百万美元的漏洞。简而言之，发生了什么攻击者首先存入相对较小的金额（大约 10 万至 20 万美元的 USDC），然后用它与 Resolv 的 USR 稳定币铸币系统进行交互。通常情况下，用户存入 USDC 并收到等值的 USR 作为回报。然而，在这种情况下，攻击者能够铸造大约 8000 万个 USR 代币，远远超出了他们的存款应允许的范围。这是可能的，因为铸造批准取决于链下服务，该服务使用特权私钥来签署可以创建多少 USR。不幸的是，智能合约本身并没有对铸造施加任何最大限​​制——它只是检查是否存在有效签名。在铸造无支持的 USR 后，攻击者迅速将其转换为质押版本（wstUSR），然后逐渐将其交换为其他稳定币，最终交换为 ETH。到攻击结束时，他们已经提取了大约 2500 万美元的 ETH。无支持的 USR 突然涌入市场也导致代币价格下跌了 80% 左右。在结果已知的情况下，让我们快速了解一下铸造设计如何使这次黑客攻击成为可能。Resolv 的代币铸造应该如何工作要了解这种攻击是如何发生的，需要首先了解 Resolv 的铸造设计。当用户想要铸造 Resolv 的原生代币 USR 时，他们不会与自治链上机制进行交互。相反，他们会经历一个两步的链下流程： requestSwap – 用户将 USDC 存入 USR Counter 合约并提交铸币请求。 CompleteSwap – 一项链下服务，由名为 SERVICE_ROLE 的特权私钥控制，审查请求并回调合约以最终确定要铸造多少 USR。该合同强制执行最低的 USR 产量——但关键的是，没有最高产量。存入的抵押品和要铸造的 USR 之间没有链上比率检查。没有价格神谕。没有上限。没有最大薄荷比率。因此，无论密钥持有者签署什么，都会被铸造。攻击的逐步分解步骤 1. 访问 Resolv 的 AWS KMS 环境攻击者破坏了 Resolv 的云基础设施，以访问存储协议特权签名密钥的 Resolv 的 AWS Key Management Service (KMS) 环境。通过控制 KMS 环境，攻击者可以使用 Resolv 自己的铸币密钥来授权他们选择的任何铸币操作。 步骤 2. 铸造 USR 代币 攻击者使用签名密钥发出两个交换请求，每个请求都通过少量交易提供总计约 10 万至 20 万美元的适度 USDC 存款。然后使用 SERVICE_ROLE 密钥以夸大的输出金额调用completeSwap，授权数千万 USR 换取 USDC 存款。链上已识别出两笔主要交易： 5000 万 USR 铸造交易 3000 万 USR 铸造交易 总共铸造了 8000 万个 USR 代币，约 2500 万美元。 步骤 3. 使用 wstUSR 绕过流动性攻击者随后将 USR 转换为 wstUSR （包装质押 USR），一种衍生代币，代表质押池的份额，而不是固定数量的 USR。通过质押 wstUSR，攻击者将其头寸从一种会立即导致市场陷入困境的形式转移到流动性较低但更具可替代性的衍生品。第 4 步：从 wstUSR 兑现，攻击者兑换成稳定币，然后兑换成 ETH，通过多个 DEX 池和桥进行轮换，以最大限度地提取资金并掩盖踪迹。在撰写本文时，攻击者的钱包持有：约 11,400 ETH（约 2400 万美元）约 2000 万美元wstUSR（价格低迷时约为 130 万美元） 对 USR 持有者来说，后果是直接而严重的。8000 万个新铸造的、无支持的代币开始流入 DEX 流动性池。随着供应涌入市场，美元与美元挂钩的汇率崩溃了。该代币曾一度跌至 0.20 美元（暴跌 80%），随后部分回升至 0.56 美元左右。攻击发生后，Resolv Labs 发表声明，暂停所有协议功能以防止进一步损害，并开始调查此次违规行为。防止进一步损害的紧迫性也再强烈不过了，尤其是当攻击者试图铸造更多货币时，因此对此类攻击尽可能立即做出反应的重要性。Hexagate 如何保护 ResolvResolv 上的黑客行为是实时链上监控旨在捕获的内容的一个很好的例子。使用 Chainaanalysis Hexagate，可以使用两种具体的检测方法：选项 1：监控异常铸造事件 像 Hexagate 这样的监控系统可以配置为监视任何 CompleteSwap 函数调用，其中铸造的 USR 输出与存入的抵押品输入不成比例。授权 5000 万 USR 的 10 万美元 USDC 存款是任何合法用户都不会生成的异常情况。此函数调用模式的警报如果标记比率高于正常范围的 1.5 倍，则会立即标记两个主要交易。Hexagate 的定制监视器将在检测到利用 Resolv 铸币机制的铸币异常时触发自动响应。选项 2：为这一关键合约事件提供自定义功能的 Gatesigner 攻击者必须经历 requestSwap →completeSwap 流程，该流程在每个阶段都会生成链上事件。 Hexagate 的 GateSigner 与合约事件监控相结合，可以配置为检测异常的 Mint 事件，并在 8000 万 USR 中的一美元到达公开市场之前自动触发合约暂停。良好的安全性意味着假设会出现问题虽然 Resolv 已经采取了所有经典的安全措施，并进行了多达 18 次审计，但从某种意义上说，Resolv 上的黑客攻击是一个简单的故事：攻击者获得了密钥，用它来印钞票，然后出售假钞但从更深层的意义上来说，这是一个关于 DeFi 协议如何继承其所依赖的链下基础设施的安全假设和漏洞的故事。链上智能合约完美运行。受损密钥的更广泛的系统设计和链下基础设施显然没有做到这一点。实时监控和自动响应机制现在是必需品，而不是奢侈品，因为漏洞利用在几分钟内展开，一旦损坏可见，就没有时间采取反应措施。详细了解 Hexagate 的实时链上威胁检测监视器和自动响应如何防止您成为下一次大抢劫的受害者，或立即请求演示。

Original Summary

  1. On March 22, 2026, the Resolv DeFi protocol became the latest example of how quickly things can unravel in DeFi when security assumptions fail. In a matter of minutes, an attacker was able to mint tens of millions of Resolv’s unbacked stablecoins (USR) and extract roughly $23 million in value, triggering a sharp de-peg and forcing the protocol to halt operations.At first glance, this might look like another smart contract exploit. But it wasn’t. The code worked exactly as intended. Instead, it was a case of overly trusting off-chain infrastructure. As DeFi systems become more complex and use more external services, privileged keys, and cloud infrastructure, the attack surface expands far beyond the blockchain itself.In this post, we’ll look at what happened and what the impact was. We’ll also explore how when off-chain components are compromised, only real-time, on-chain threat detection and response mechanisms can act as the critical final line of defence and make the difference between a contained incident and a multi-million dollar exploit.What happened, in a nutshellThe attacker started by depositing a relatively small amount (around $100K–$200K in USDC) and used it to interact with Resolv’s USR stablecoin minting system. Normally, users deposit USDC and receive an equivalent amount of USR in return. However, in this case, the attacker was able to mint around 80 million USR tokens, far beyond what their deposit should have allowed.This was possible because minting approvals depended on an off-chain service that used a privileged private key to sign off on how much USR could be created. Unfortunately, the smart contract itself did not enforce any maximum limit on minting – it only checked that a valid signature existed.After minting the unbacked USR, the attacker quickly converted it into a staked version (wstUSR), then gradually swapped it into other stablecoins and eventually into ETH. By the end of the attack, they had extracted approximately $25 million in ETH. The sudden flood of unbacked USR into the market also caused the token’s price to drop by around 80%.With the outcome known, let’s take a quick look at how the minting design made this hack possible.How Resolv’s token minting is supposed to workUnderstanding how this attack happened requires first understanding Resolv’s minting design.When a user wants to mint Resolv’s native token, USR, they don’t interact with an autonomous on-chain mechanism. Instead, they go through a two-step off-chain process: requestSwap – The user deposits USDC into the USR Counter contract and submits a minting request. completeSwap – An off-chain service, controlled by a privileged private key called the SERVICE_ROLE, reviews the request and calls back to the contract to finalize how much USR to mint. The contract enforces a minimum USR output – but critically, no maximum. There is no on-chain ratio check between the collateral deposited and the USR to be minted. No price oracle. No cap. No maximum mint ratio. So, whatever the key holder signs will get minted.A step by step breakdown of the attackStep 1. Gaining Access to Resolv’s AWS KMS EnvironmentThe attacker compromised Resolv’s cloud infrastructure to gain access to Resolv’s AWS Key Management Service (KMS) environment where the protocol’s privileged signing key was stored. With control over the KMS environment, the attacker could use Resolv’s own minting key to authorize any minting operation they chose.Step 2. Minting the USR TokensArmed with the signing key, the attacker made two swap requests, each funded with a modest USDC deposit totaling approximately $100K – $200K across a handful of transactions. The SERVICE_ROLE key was then used to call completeSwap with inflated output amounts, authorizing tens of millions of USR in exchange for the USDC deposits.Two primary transactions have been identified on-chain: 50M USR minting transaction 30M USR minting transaction In total, 80 million USR tokens were minted, approximately $25 million.Step 3. Bypassing Liquidity with wstUSRThe attacker then converted USR into wstUSR (wrapped staked USR), a derivative token that represents a share of the staking pool rather than a fixed number of USR. By staking into wstUSR, the attacker moved their position out of a form that would immediately tank the market and into a less liquid but more fungible derivative.Step 4. Cashing OutFrom wstUSR, the attacker swapped into stablecoins, then into ETH, rotating through multiple DEX pools and bridges to maximize their extraction and obscure the trail.At the time of writing, the attacker’s wallet holds: ~11,400 ETH (~$24 million) ~20 million wstUSR (~$1.3 million at depressed prices) The consequences for USR holders were immediate and severe.The 80 million newly minted, unbacked tokens began hitting DEX liquidity pools. As the supply flooded the markets, USR’s dollar peg collapsed. The token dropped as low as $0.20 (an 80% collapse) before recovering partially to around $0.56 in the hours that followed.Following the attack, Resolv Labs issued a statement and suspended all protocol functions to prevent further damage and began investigating the breach. The urgency to prevent further damage couldn’t have been stronger either, especially as the attacker was trying to mint even more, hence the importance of having as an immediate response as possible to such an attack.How Hexagate could have protected ResolvThe hack on Resolv is a good example of what real-time on-chain monitoring is designed to catch. With Chainalysis Hexagate, two concrete detection approaches would have been available:Option 1: Monitor for anomalous minting eventsA monitoring system like Hexagate could have been configured to watch for any completeSwap function call where the minted USR output was disproportionate to the deposited collateral input.A $100K USDC deposit authorizing 50 million USR is an anomaly that no legitimate user would ever generate. An alert on this function call pattern with a threshold that flags ratios above, say, 1.5x the normal range, would have flagged both primary transactions instantly.Hexagate’s customized monitor that would have triggered an automated response upon detecting the minting anomaly that exploited Resolv’s minting mechanism.Option 2: Gatesigner with custom functionality for this critical contract eventThe attacker had to go through the requestSwap → completeSwap flow, and this flow generates on-chain events at every stage. Hexagate’s GateSigner combined with contract event monitoring could have been configured to detect the anomalous Mint event and automatically trigger a contract pause before a single dollar of the 80 million USR reached the open market.Good security means assuming something will go wrongWhile Resolv had undergone all the classic security measures, and had undergone as many as 18 audits, the hack on Resolv is, in one sense, a simple story: an attacker got a key, used it to print money, and sold the fake money before anyone noticed.But in a deeper sense, it’s a story about how DeFi protocols inherit the security assumptions, and the vulnerabilities, of the off-chain infrastructure they depend on. The on-chain smart contract worked perfectly. The broader system design and off-chain infrastructure of the compromised key apparently did not.Real-time monitoring and automated response mechanisms are now a necessity, not a luxury, as exploits unfold in minutes, leaving no time for reactive measures once the damage is visible.Learn more about how Hexagate’s real-time on-chain threat detection monitors and automated response can prevent you from being a victim of the next big heist, or request a demo today.

primitivesuave 2026/03/24 07:18
文章中没有提及——黑客首先入侵了 Resolv Lab 的 AWS 账户，从 KMS 获取了用于控制铸币的私钥，然后在所有协议功能暂停之前成功将 2500 万美元提取到 ETH 中。
Missing from the article - the hacker first compromised Resolv Lab's AWS account, took a private key from KMS that was used to control minting, then managed to extract $25 million into ETH before all protocol functions were suspended.

WatchDog 2026/03/24 12:44
> 从 KMS 获取私钥

他们使用 KMS 对铸币操作进行签名，但他们没有“获取”密钥，AWS KMS 不允许您提取密钥。

> took a private key from KMS

They used KMS to sign the minting operation, but they didn't "take" the key, AWS KMS doesn't let you extract keys.

在 HN 中查看
pants2 2026/03/24 13:43
^ 这是加密领域常见的安全误解。 “我们使用的是 HSM，他们无法窃取我们的私钥。”好的天才，现在您仍然需要保护 HSM。

拥有 3 个以上密钥持有者的 MPC/多重签名没有捷径。

^ this is a common security misconception in crypto. "We're using an HSM, they can't steal our private key." OK genius now you still have to secure the HSM.

There's no shortcut to MPC/multisig with 3+ keyholders.

Aurornis 2026/03/24 07:34
根据一篇文章 https://www.chainalysis.com/blog/lessons-from-the-resolv-hac... 这始于一次普通的旧黑客攻击，破坏了他们的签名密钥。

他们还有一个智能合约，但没有进行一些适当的检查，但只有使用被盗的私钥才能进行黑客攻击。持有私钥的人都可以不受限制地铸造大量货币。

因此，这次抢劫的核心是传统的黑客攻击，而不仅仅是智能合约漏洞。

According to a writeup at https://www.chainalysis.com/blog/lessons-from-the-resolv-hac... this started with a plain old hack that compromised their signing key.

They also had a smart contract which didn't do some proper checks, but the hack was only possible with the stolen private key. Whoever held the private key was able to mint a lot of money, unchecked.

So there was a traditional hack at the core of this heist, not just a smart contract exploit.

bravoetch 2026/03/24 08:32

Usually I would expect proof for a positive - like that it was an inside job, or there being an indication of it. I'm not saying whether it was or not, just that it seems unusual for you to ask about proof of it NOT being an inside job.

kibwen 2026/03/24 08:45
当谈到加密货币时，不，被证明是内部工作拉扯的“黑客”是如此普遍，以至于正确的举证责任落在了认为这一点的人身上 不是 内部工作。
When it comes to crypticurrencies, no, the "hack" that turns out to be an inside-job rugpull is so common that the correct burden of proof is on the people who think this wasn't an inside job.

mothballed 2026/03/24 08:22
大多数人都没有意识到自己身处有机玻璃屏蔽的金融监狱中，直到他们尝试在某个敏感的地方或 FATF 灰名单上为某些合法活动汇钱之类的事情。

如果你属于中等用途，或者属于可以改变或制定规则的上层阶级，那么金融体系就运转良好，看起来质疑它的人只是怪人。

确实，外围群体中的许多人都是罪犯，但其他人则是“购买一辆卡车，在恐怖分子领土外为饥饿的伊拉克儿童建造孤儿院”或“想要在某个腐败的岛屿天堂获得投资签证，但事实证明，没有银行会为‘通往科摩罗的国际电汇’开设账户”。

Most people don't realize they're inside a plexiglass shielded financial jail until they try to do something like wiring money for some legal activity in someplace spicy or on the FATF grey list.

If you fall into the middle bands of uses, or in the upper class that can just bend or make the rules, then the financial system is well oiled and it looks like the people questioning it are just cranks.

It's true that a lot of those in the outer bands are criminals but others are things like "buying a truck to build an orphanage for starving Iraqi children just outside of terrorist territory" or "wanted an investment visa in some corrupt island paradise and as it turns out no bank will open up account for purposes of 'international wires to the Comoros' "

troad 2026/03/24 08:55

在 HN 中查看
mothballed 2026/03/24 08:58
这两件事中的一件非常接近发生在我身上的事情。 我试图开设一个银行账户，用于支付到某个特定国家的移民相关费用，这既合法，又是完全合法努力的一部分，但没有银行愿意这样做。

另一个例子有点捏造，但植根于我在伊拉克度过的时间，并注意到几乎所有交易都是在银行系统之外进行的，部分原因是银行业务无法访问，人们通常无法访问 KYC 文件。

这确实不荒唐。 一旦你开始尝试做任何有趣的事情，KYC/AML 负担就会变得更大，直到最终你意识到合规官员只是想让你离开（或者直接拒绝你），变得足够有趣，然后突然间，尽管你完全遵守法律，但你发现围墙在你周围关闭。 大多数人永远不会发现，因为他们从来没有机会尝试，他们做了一堆无聊的国内交易，再加上一些与一些知名实体的国际贸易，然后他们只是大喊人们在编造荒谬的事情。

One of the two is very close to something that actually happened to me. I tried to open up a bank account for paying immigration related costs to a particular shithole country, which is both legal and was part of a fully legal endeavor, but no bank would do it.

The other example is somewhat concocted but rooted in the time I spent in Iraq and noting almost all transactions are performed outside the banking system, in part because banking is inaccessible and people often don't have access to KYC documents.

It's really not absurd. As soon as you start trying to do anything interesting the KYC/AML burdens get greater until eventually you realize the compliance officers are just trying to get you to go away (or just deny you outright), get interesting enough and then suddenly despite fully complying with the law you find the walls are closed around you. Most people never find out because they never have occasion to try, they do a bunch of boring domestic transactions plus maybe some international trade with a few well known entities, then they just shout people are making up absurdities.

在 HN 中查看
troad 2026/03/24 09:15
显然，您试图通过投资获得科摩罗居留权的情况会让银行感到惊讶，因为银行的职责是监控税务合规性。我不认为你描述的是普通人的情况。

我也不完全明白你为什么要向银行合理化账户的用途。您不能为了任何目的而开设一个帐户吗？我需要五分钟才能在网上开设一个账户，而且我从来没有被要求解释或证明任何事情（几十年来）。我经常使用我的账户，包括国际转账（过去四年我住在两大洲）。我什至曾经用普通银行账户支付了去朝鲜的旅行费用。我的银行从来没有眨过眼睛。

也许您只是在与一家坏账银行打交道，或者是一个过度监管的银行系统（欧洲？）。您是否意识到您现在可以走进任何一家美国银行，他们只需提供一些准确的身份证件即可为您开设账户？世界其他大部分地区也是如此吗？您试图解决的问题已经解决了。

>> 另一个例子有点捏造，但植根于我在伊拉克度过的时间，并注意到几乎所有交易都是在银行系统之外进行的，部分原因是银行业务无法访问，人们通常无法访问 KYC 文件。

缺乏经验的半文盲农民是最后一个合理期望开设加密货币经纪账户并开始交易合成美元衍生品的人群。

这些都不是现实的场景。当人们绞尽脑汁试图找出稳定币可能有用的原因时，他们就会这么说。我觉得你只是在确认它们是寻找问题的解决方案。

Clearly your situation of trying to obtain residency in the Comoros by investment would raise eyebrows at banks whose job it is to monitor tax compliance. I don't think you're describing an everyman kind of scenario.

I also don't entirely understand why you're even rationalising the purpose of the account to the bank. Can't you just open an account for any purpose? It takes me five minutes to open an account online, and I've never once been asked to explain or justify anything (in many decades). I use my accounts robustly, including for international transfers (I've lived on two continents in the last four years). I even once paid for a trip to North Korea out of an ordinary bank account. My bank never batted an eye.

Maybe you're just dealing with a bad bank, or an over-regulated banking system (Europe?). You realise you can walk into any US bank right now and they'll just open an account for you with nothing more than some accurate ID? And the same holds for much of the rest of the world? The problem you're trying to solve is already solved.

>> The other example is somewhat concocted but rooted in the time I spent in Iraq and noting almost all transactions are performed outside the banking system, in part because banking is inaccessible and people often don't have access to KYC documents.

Unsophisticated semi-literate farmers are the last demographic anyone is reasonably expecting to open their crypto brokerage accounts and start trading synthetic USD derivatives.

These are just not realistic scenarios. This is what people say when they rack their brains trying to come up with some reason stablecoins might be useful. I feel like you're just confirming that they're a solution in search of a problem.

Saline9515 2026/03/24 08:22
稳定币可以实现类似现金（可立即兑换且可验证）的大额支付，且几乎免费。

在欧盟国家，你现在不能用现金购买汽车。您必须从银行购买不记名支票，价格昂贵，要求双方都有实体银行，而且不能跨境操作。稳定币解决了这个问题。

Stablecoins enable cash-like (instantly redeemable and verifiable) payments for large amounts, for almost free.

In EU countries, you can't now buy a car with cash. You have to buy a bearer's check from your bank, which is expensive, requires that both parties have a brick and mortar bank, and doesn't work cross-border. Stablecoins solve this.

Saline9515 2026/03/24 08:24

在 HN 中查看
anonym29 2026/03/24 07:51
>我的意思是他们使用区块链，对吧？这难道不是名称“crypto”的唯一真正要求吗？

绝对不是。加密货币目前专门指未经许可、去中心化、加密保护、不可逆转、可替代的货币体系，具有通货紧缩或非通货膨胀的供应，遵循自愿、集体化的治理模式。

绝大多数通俗地称为“加密货币”的代币都背离了这些原则。不存在属于加密货币的稳定币。以太坊不是加密货币。公司发行的任何硬币（例如瑞波币）都不是加密货币。

>I mean they use Blockchain, right? Isn't that like the only real requirement for the name crypto?

Absolutely not. Cryptocurrently exclusively refers to permissionless, decentralized, cryptographically secured, irreversible, fungible monetary system with a disinflationary or non-inflationary supply, following a voluntary, collectivized governance model.

A vast majority of tokens colloquially referred to as "cryptocurrency" couldn't be further from these principles. There are no stablecoins that are cryptocurrency. Ethereum is not cryptocurrency. Any coin issued by a corporation (e.g. Ripple) is not a cryptocurrency.

在 HN 中查看
YawningAngel 2026/03/24 08:38
如果您的定义排除了以太坊，那么您对该术语的理解与其他人的理解不同，因此我们谈论的不是同一件事
If your definition excludes Ethereum your understanding of the term so differs from everyone else's that we aren't talking about the same thing
在 HN 中查看
anonym29 2026/03/24 09:19
以太坊是一个很棒的实用代币。智能合约在数字经济中绝对有用。它只是不是一种加密货币，仅此而已。它进行了大量预挖，没有供应上限，受到 OFAC 审查，并且有效地证明，只有 ETH 总供应量的 4.8% 左右可以投票推动推出和广泛采用可逆转交易的分叉。

我们需要用不同的词来形容这些根本不同的事物，因为将它们混为一谈会导致真正的混乱，正如这个黑客所证明的那样。人们对管理员能够锁定交易感到惊讶，因为“加密货币”这个词让他们假设了稳定币中不存在的属性。

Ethereum is a great utility token. Smart contracts absolutely have utility in the digital economy. It's just not a cryptocurrency, is all. It had a massive premine, there's no supply cap, it's subject to OFAC censorship, and has effectively demonstrated that just ~4.8% of the total ETH supply can vote to cause rollout and widespread adoption of a fork that reverses transactions.

We need different words for these fundamentally different things, because conflating them causes real confusion, as this very hack demonstrates. People are surprised that an admin can lock transactions precisely because the word "cryptocurrency" led them to assume properties that don't exist in stablecoins.

Currency isn't a homebrew computer or backyard car project; it is either centralised or not; there is no in between.

Blockchain with central authority is the worst of both worlds.

在 HN 中查看
0x3f 2026/03/24 07:31
并不真地。 在传统银行，我必须信任 n 个拥有不同访问权限的人。 在其他条件不变的情况下，n 的任何减少都是一种改进，即使 n 不为零。

当然，n 可以更小，特定的人也可以更不值得信任，但这是完全不同的事情。

Not really. At a traditional bank I have to trust n people with varying degrees of access. Et ceteris paribus, any reduction in n is an improvement, even if n is not zero.

Of course n can be smaller and the specific people less trustworthy, but that's quite a different thing.

在 HN 中查看
mnkyprskbd 2026/03/24 07:38
在传统银行，您有国家存款保险计划；作为将你的“资产”转换为上述国家发行的货币的回报，你会得到这一点，但接受当局对货币供应和你的资金的控制。

通过去中心化货币，您可以获得由密码学支持的全球分布式证明的安全性，而无需单一机构控制货币或您的资金的供应。

没有半途而废的选择。您要么拥有可以行使控制权的单一权力，要么没有；由于您可以更换银行，因此行使控制权的代表人数几乎无关紧要。

At a traditional bank you have your national deposit insurance scheme; you get that in return for converting your "assets" to the said nations issued currency but accept the authorities control of the money supply and your funds.

With decentralised money, you get the safety of a globally distributed attestation backed by cryptography without a single authority controlling the supply of money or your funds.

There is no halfway option. You either have a single authority that can exercise control or you do not; number of delegates for exercise of control is almost irrelevant since you can change banks.

在 HN 中查看
ribosometronome 2026/03/24 07:53

dylan604 2026/03/24 08:09
是否受到 FDIC 法律追索权的承保？
is insured by the FDIC legal recourse?
在 HN 中查看
mothballed 2026/03/24 08:11
FDIC 不承保银行盗窃[]。

  FDIC deposit insurance does not protect against losses due to theft or fraud, which are addressed by other laws.
这由私人银行家债券保险承保，就像您可以购买分散存储的黄金罐一样，或者您可以以看跌期权（如 IBIT）的形式购买比特币价值损失的保险，或者如果您的冷钱包被盗，您可以对窃贼提起法律诉讼。

[] https://www.fdic.gov/news/fact-sheets/crypto-fact-sheet-7-28...

FDIC does not cover bank theft[].

  FDIC deposit insurance does not protect against losses due to theft or fraud, which are addressed by other laws.
That's covered by private bankers bond insurance, much like you could get for a decentralized stored pots of gold or you can buy insurance in the form of put options (like on IBIT) on the loss of value of bitcoin or if your cold wallet is stolen you can initiate legal proceedings against the thief.

[] https://www.fdic.gov/news/fact-sheets/crypto-fact-sheet-7-28...

在 HN 中查看
dylan604 2026/03/24 09:13
很高兴知道这一点。我认为这是有道理的，因为那些被麦道夫欺骗的人必须通过麦道夫的遗产而不是联邦存款保险公司收回他们的钱。

我想好莱坞在几乎每一个银行抢劫场景中都再次误导了我们，比如“没人惊慌。我们不是在偷你的钱，我们是在偷银行的钱”。

babypuncher 2026/03/24 13:37
加密货币的主要卖点都取决于它们是去中心化的并且不能由单一实体控制的承诺。如果没有这些，它们只是新版本的 PayPal 或信用卡网络，需要更多数量级的计算资源来维护。
The primary selling points of cryptocurrencies are all hinged on the promise that they are decentralized and can't be controlled by a single entity. Without that, all they are is a new version of PayPal or a credit card network that requires many orders of magnitude more compute resources to maintain.

cameldrv 2026/03/24 12:11
您不应该拥有控制云服务上数百万/数十亿美元的密钥。 它应该位于匿名购买的气隙笔记本电脑上，从未连接到互联网，并且仅运行经过审查并通过 CD-ROM 或其他类似方法加载到其上的软件。
You shouldn't have a key that controls millions/billions of dollars on a cloud service. It should be on an airgapped laptop that was purchased anonymously, has never been connected to the Internet, and only runs software that has been vetted and loaded onto it via a CD-ROM or some other comparable method.

If their coin requires a web service to process each transaction, then an offline key isn't very useful.

You can criticize their design, but you can't have a dude burning a CD-ROM every time someone wants some coins.

amarant 2026/03/24 07:32
稳定币的意义何在？比如为什么有人买它们？

在我看来，它们的初始价值是每个代币 1 美元（或者我猜的其他一些法定货币），这也是它们价值的顶峰：它们有点保证它们不会变得比这个更有价值。

它们的可用性不如法定货币：更多的企业接受法定货币而不是加密货币，尤其是像所有稳定币一样的怪异和小硬币。

正如这里所示，它们的价值并没有真正的底线。

我看到拥有其中一枚硬币有很多缺点，但没有一个优点？

但人们显然确实购买了它们，那么有什么好处呢？他们身上肯定有什么好的地方吧？

What is the point of stable coins? Like why does anyone buy them?

It seems to me that their initial value is 1usd per token (or some other fiat I guess) and that's also the roof of their value: they kinda guarantee that they won't become more valuable than that.

They are less usable than fiat: more businesses accept fiat than crypto, especially weird and small coins like all stable coins are.

There isn't really a floor to their value, as demonstrated here.

I see plenty of downsides of owning one of these coins, but not a single upside?

Yet people apparently do buy them, so what is the upside? There must surely be something that's good about them?

在 HN 中查看
fintech_eng 2026/03/24 07:52
它们并不是真的要升值。

主要用途是拥有类似美元的东西，您可以轻松移动。这在美国境外很有用，但对于美国境内的很多人来说也很有用，具体取决于他们在做什么；尤其是那些很难获得或维持正常银行业务的企业（咳嗽赌博、色情、大麻咳嗽）。

它们在加密货币中很方便，因为您可以在不接触银行的情况下移入/移出其他资产。有时你可以从它们身上赚取收益，这是吸引力的一部分（带有通常的“这可能会爆炸”的警告）。

此外，每家公司都想推出稳定币是有原因的：如果你控制了稳定币，你就可以获得浮动和轨道。如果人们真正使用它，那将是一项非常好的生意。

如果您已经可以稳定地使用美元并且不关心这种灵活性，那么它们就不那么有吸引力了。

但是，是的，并非没有风险（脱钩、发行人风险等）。老实说，除了商业激励之外，可能没有太多真正需要数十个略有不同的马厩。

They’re not really meant to go up in value.

The main use is just having something dollar-like that you can move around easily. That’s useful outside the US, but also for plenty of people inside the US depending on what they’re doing; especially businesses that have a hard time getting or keeping normal banking (cough gambling, porn, weed cough).

They’re handy inside crypto since you can move in/out of other assets without touching a bank. And sometimes you can earn yield on them, which is part of the appeal (with the usual “this can blow up” caveats).

Also, there’s a reason every company wants to launch one: if you control the stablecoin, you get the float and the rails. That’s a pretty nice business if people actually use it.

If you already have solid access to USD and don’t care about that flexibility, they’re less compelling.

But yeah, not risk-free at all (depegs, issuer risk, etc). And honestly there probably isn’t much real need for dozens of slightly different stables beyond the business incentives.

The ones I use are several orders of magnitude less friction and most are 100% free. The ones that do have a cost (for recipients outside Scandinavia basically) are still way, waay cheaper than crypto transactions.

stevage 2026/03/24 08:29
我认为这个想法是，如果您尝试以通常使用金钱的方式实际使用加密货币（即购买/出售东西），那么您不希望出现波动性。因此从理论上讲，它消除了加密生态系统中的波动性。

但显然……事情发生了。就像现金通常相对不易波动一样，但金融崩溃却时有发生。

I think the idea is if you're attempting to actually use crypto in the way that you would normally use money (ie, to buy/sell stuff) then you don't want the volatility. So in theory, it takes away the volatility while living within the crypto ecosystem.

But obviously...things happen. Just like cash is usually relatively non-volatile, but financial crashes happen.

Has to be an inside job. One doesn’t just simultaneously hack into an AWS account, know exactly which key is needed for coin minting, and know internal details necessary to exploit a smart contract. The nature of the hack practically reveals their identity.

le-mark 2026/03/24 07:11
Tl;dr 智能合约中的另一个漏洞被利用，黑客却全身而退。
Tl;dr another bug in a smart contract exploited, hacker got away clean.
在 HN 中查看
MrDrone 2026/03/24 08:04
虽然这并不重要，但这个总结并不正确。合同没有被“利用”。该公司的 AWS 账户遭到泄露，攻击者可以访问（链下）私钥。

该合约依赖于密钥来铸造新代币。黑客（通过 AWS）获得了密钥的访问权限，并根据需要铸造了任意数量的密钥。只需要私钥来铸造无限量代币的合约并不是一个好的合约，这当然是一个有效的观点，但你不能通过从迎宾垫下抓住钥匙来利用某人的前门锁。

Not that it matters much, but this summary isn't right. The contract wasn't "exploited." The company's AWS account was compromised, giving the attacker access to a (off-chain) private key.

The contract relied on the key to mint new tokens. The hacker gained access to the key (through AWS) and with it minted as much as they'd like. It is certainly a valid take that a contract that only required the private key to mint an unlimited amount of the token isn't a good one, but you don't exploit someone's front door lock by grabbing the key from under the welcome mat.

dafelst 2026/03/24 07:12

But guys, what you don't understand is that the code IS the contract!!! That means you don't even NEED regulation!!
在 HN 中查看
0x3f 2026/03/24 07:29
是的，那些真正相信这一点的人对于智能合约被利用没有任何问题。 当然，有些人这么说是因为当时经济上有利，但后来又改变了态度。 但这两个群体都存在，这并不是一个真正的陷阱。
Yeah, people who genuinely believe that don't have any problem with smart contracts getting exploited. Of course there are people who _say_ that because it's financially expedient at the time, then change their tune. But both groups exist and this is not really a gotcha.
在 HN 中查看
protocolture 2026/03/24 08:02
我不介意智能合约接受战斗考验。

我也不介意整个链条聚集在一起投票推翻交易。

我也不介意一群人对此不满意并分叉。

I dont mind smart contracts getting battle tested.

I also dont mind the whole chain coming together to vote to reverse the transaction.

I also dont mind a bunch of people being unhappy with that and forking.

在 HN 中查看
MrDrone 2026/03/24 08:07
合约代码说：“如果你有一个有效的（链外）私钥，你就可以铸造代币。”黑客获得了他们的 AWS 账户并最终获得了密钥。

虽然我很高兴庆祝愚蠢的加密货币，但这并不是某人的代码被“利用”的情况。他们的代码很愚蠢，仅依赖链外私钥来允许铸造代币。他们的安全也很糟糕。

The contract code said, "if you have a valid (off-chain) private key, you can mint tokens." The hacker gained access to their AWS account and ultimately their keys.

While I am happy to celebrate dumb crypto stuff, this isn't a situation where someone's code was "exploited." Their code was stupid, relying only on an off-chain private key to allow the minting of tokens. Their security was just also bad.
